story:
Jessie Macrae lives by the birch woods, where the wind carries her song across the meadows. She weaves grasses into bright baskets as her father works in Gairloch. She is small and merry and easily bored, rolling in the fields until the neighbor, Logan, calls her home. Saoirse, the neighbor with a smile like the blue sky warmed by sunlight, praises Jessie’s baskets and asks about the market. Jessie promises to tell her all about it next time.

Her father, Coinneach, is pine and evergreen, his voice warm and rough like a fire just catching. He loves stories of the Highlands, and he loves Jessie more. But when she asks to go to the market, his face hardens: the woods aren’t safe. Jessie protests; he raises his voice for once, then falters, exhausted. Something is wrong—his hands tremble, and when Jessie asks after her mother, his voice breaks. He clings to her. You can’t go. I can’t live without you. Jessie, good at obeying, softens and holds him as he cries.

By morning, he relents. The two set off with a wooden cart rattling over roots and stones. Jessie’s baskets sit snug among onions and carrots. She watches the moss climbing the birch trunks, admires the brave scratches on her father’s arms, and thinks she wants to be just like him. Gairloch appears, salt air and pointy rooftops peeking over the hills. The market glitters. Baskets vanish into happy hands. Jessie helps call out to customers, and her father’s laugh warms her like the sun. I love you, Papa. I love you too, A bhobain.

They start home late, the sky still holding daylight the way summer does. The path is clear, white bark catching the last of the light. Jessie runs ahead. Coinneach can’t reach her; the cart is heavy. Don’t go too far! She turns to smile—and he is suddenly out of sight. The trees look the same in every direction. She calls like Logan taught her for sheep, tries not to cry, fails. The woods amber over to orange; the orange deepens to purple. She curls against a birch and sobs.

The ground shivers. Branches lift like curtains. A quiet voice. Are you alright? A long, wild head of hair, a hand the size of a tree trunk’s heart, and a cloak of moss and leaves. The Ghillie Dhu, he says, soft and careful, as if he might scare her by speaking too loud. Jessie sniffs and nods. I’m lost. He lays a hand across her back, weight gentle as a blanket. You’ll be alright. Hold on tight. He carries her through the dimming woods toward home.

On her doorstep, Jessie sleeps in his arms. He sets her gently on the threshold. A shout splits the night. Coinneach bursts from the dark with a torch and a search party. “Away from her!” he roars. “I know what you did—found her crying against a birch, and carried her here!” Rakes and flames surge, fear sharpening into cruelty. The Ghillie Dhu startles; fire licks moss and hair; he wails like a wounded stag. Jessie wakes, reaching toward him. He helped me! Please don’t! Her father hesitates, seeing a monster and a victim at once, then gathers Jessie and takes her inside. This is why we don’t wander off, he says, stroking her hair. When she asks why he hurt the creature even though he knew he’d helped, he calls the Ghillie Dhu a beast and tells her not to get attached.

Outside, the wails carry on the summer wind. Inside, a child cries herself to sleep, her father slumped beside her bed, drained by a grief he still cannot name aloud. Jessie whispers into her blanket, small and aching: How cruel we are. Please wait for me. The night holds both innocence and suffering, side by side, and the birch woods remember everything.
